name: pyro5

metadata.name: Pyro5
metadata.summary: Remote object communication library, fifth major version

documentation:
# Pyro5

*Remote objects communication library*

```{image} https://img.shields.io/pypi/v/Pyro5.svg
:target: https://pypi.python.org/pypi/Pyro5
```

```{image} https://anaconda.org/conda-forge/pyro5/badges/version.svg
:target: https://anaconda.org/conda-forge/pyro5
```

## Info

Pyro enables you to build applications in which
objects can talk to each other over the network, with minimal programming effort.
You can just use normal Python method calls, and Pyro takes care of locating the right object on the right
computer to execute the method. It is designed to be very easy to use, and to
stay out of your way. But it also provides a set of powerful features that
enables you to build distributed applications rapidly and effortlessly.
Pyro is a pure Python library and runs on many different platforms and Python versions.

Pyro is copyright © Irmen de Jong (<mailto:irmen@razorvine.net> | <http://www.razorvine.net>).  Please read the file `license`.

Pyro can be found on Pypi as [Pyro5](http://pypi.python.org/pypi/Pyro5/).  Source is on Github: <https://github.com/irmen/Pyro5>
Documentation is here: <https://pyro5.readthedocs.io/>

Pyro5 is the current version of Pyro. [Pyro4](https://pyro4.readthedocs.io/) is the predecessor
that only gets important bugfixes and security fixes, but is otherwise no longer being improved.
New code should use Pyro5 if at all possible.

## Features

- written in 100% Python so extremely portable, supported on Python 3.8 and newer, and Pypy3
- works between different system architectures and operating systems.
- able to communicate between different Python versions transparently.
- defaults to a safe serializer ([serpent](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/serpent)) that supports many Python data types.
- supports different serializers (serpent, json, marshal, msgpack).
- can use IPv4, IPv6 and Unix domain sockets.
- optional secure connections via SSL/TLS (encryption, authentication and integrity), including certificate validation on both ends (2-way ssl).
- lightweight client library available for .NET and Java native code ('Pyrolite', provided separately).
- designed to be very easy to use and get out of your way as much as possible, but still provide a lot of flexibility when you do need it.
- name server that keeps track of your object's actual locations so you can move them around transparently.
- yellow-pages type lookups possible, based on metadata tags on registrations in the name server.
- support for automatic reconnection to servers in case of interruptions.
- automatic proxy-ing of Pyro objects which means you can return references to remote objects just as if it were normal objects.
- one-way invocations for enhanced performance.
- batched invocations for greatly enhanced performance of many calls on the same object.
- remote iterator on-demand item streaming avoids having to create large collections upfront and transfer them as a whole.
- you can define timeouts on network communications to prevent a call blocking forever if there's something wrong.
- remote exceptions will be raised in the caller, as if they were local. You can extract detailed remote traceback information.
- http gateway available for clients wanting to use http+json (such as browser scripts).
- stable network communication code that has worked reliably on many platforms for over a decade.
- can hook onto existing sockets created for instance with socketpair() to communicate efficiently between threads or sub-processes.
- possibility to integrate Pyro's event loop into your own (or third party) event loop.
- three different possible instance modes for your remote objects (singleton, one per session, one per call).
- many simple examples included to show various features and techniques.
- large amount of unit tests and high test coverage.
- reliable and established: built upon more than 20 years of existing Pyro history, with ongoing support and development.

modules.name: Pyro5.svr_existingconn
documentation:
Socket server for a the special case of a single, already existing, connection.

Pyro - Python Remote Objects.  Copyright by Irmen de Jong (irmen@razorvine.net).

methods.name: Pyro5.svr_existingconn.SocketServer_ExistingConnection.handleRequest
documentation: Handles a single connection request event and returns if the connection is still active

modules.name: Pyro5.errors
documentation:
Definition of the various exceptions that are used in Pyro.

Pyro - Python Remote Objects.  Copyright by Irmen de Jong (irmen@razorvine.net).

functions.name: Pyro5.errors.get_pyro_traceback
documentation:
Returns a list of strings that form the traceback information of a
Pyro exception. Any remote Pyro exception information is included.
Traceback information is automatically obtained via `sys.exc_info()` if
you do not supply the objects yourself.

functions.name: Pyro5.errors.format_traceback
documentation:
Formats an exception traceback. If you ask for detailed formatting,
the result will contain info on the variables in each stack frame.
You don't have to provide the exception info objects, if you omit them,
this function will obtain them itself using `sys.exc_info()`.

functions.name: Pyro5.errors.excepthook
documentation: An exception hook you can use for `sys.excepthook`, to automatically print remote Pyro tracebacks

classes.name: Pyro5.errors.PyroError
documentation: Generic base of all Pyro-specific errors.

classes.name: Pyro5.errors.CommunicationError
documentation: Base class for the errors related to network communication problems.

classes.name: Pyro5.errors.ConnectionClosedError
documentation: The connection was unexpectedly closed.

classes.name: Pyro5.errors.TimeoutError
documentation:
A call could not be completed within the set timeout period,
or the network caused a timeout.

classes.name: Pyro5.errors.ProtocolError
documentation: Pyro received a message that didn't match the active Pyro network protocol, or there was a protocol related error.

classes.name: Pyro5.errors.MessageTooLargeError
documentation: Pyro received a message or was trying to send a message that exceeds the maximum message size as configured.

classes.name: Pyro5.errors.NamingError
documentation: There was a problem related to the name server or object names.

classes.name: Pyro5.errors.DaemonError
documentation: The Daemon encountered a problem.

classes.name: Pyro5.errors.SecurityError
documentation: A security related error occurred.

classes.name: Pyro5.errors.SerializeError
documentation: Something went wrong while (de)serializing data.

modules.name: Pyro5.callcontext
documentation:
Deals with the context variables of a Pyro call.

Pyro - Python Remote Objects.  Copyright by Irmen de Jong (irmen@razorvine.net).

variables.name: Pyro5.callcontext.current_context
documentation: the thread-local context object for the current Pyro call

classes.name: Pyro5.callcontext._CallContext

methods.name: Pyro5.callcontext._CallContext.track_resource
documentation: keep a weak reference to the resource to be tracked for this connection

methods.name: Pyro5.callcontext._CallContext.untrack_resource
documentation: no longer track the resource for this connection
call context thread local

modules.name: Pyro5.svr_multiplex
documentation:
Socket server based on socket multiplexing. Doesn't use threads.
Uses the best available selector (kqueue, poll, select).

Pyro - Python Remote Objects.  Copyright by Irmen de Jong (irmen@razorvine.net).

classes.name: Pyro5.svr_multiplex.SocketServer_Multiplex

methods.name: Pyro5.svr_multiplex.SocketServer_Multiplex.events
documentation: handle events that occur on one of the sockets of this server

methods.name: Pyro5.svr_multiplex.SocketServer_Multiplex.wakeup
documentation: bit of a hack to trigger a blocking server to get out of the loop, useful at clean shutdowns

methods.name: Pyro5.svr_multiplex.SocketServer_Multiplex.handleRequest
documentation: Handles a single connection request event and returns if the connection is still active
Multiplexed transport server for socket connections (uses select, poll, kqueue, ...)

modules.name: Pyro5.serializers
documentation:
The various serializers.

Pyro - Python Remote Objects.  Copyright by Irmen de Jong (irmen@razorvine.net).

classes.name: Pyro5.serializers.SerializerBase

methods.name: Pyro5.serializers.SerializerBase.register_class_to_dict
documentation:
Registers a custom function that returns a dict representation of objects of the given class.
The function is called with a single parameter; the object to be converted to a dict.

methods.name: Pyro5.serializers.SerializerBase.unregister_class_to_dict
documentation:
Removes the to-dict conversion function registered for the given class. Objects of the class
will be serialized by the default mechanism again.

methods.name: Pyro5.serializers.SerializerBase.register_dict_to_class
documentation:
Registers a custom converter function that creates objects from a dict with the given classname tag in it.
The function is called with two parameters: the classname and the dictionary to convert to an instance of the class.

methods.name: Pyro5.serializers.SerializerBase.unregister_dict_to_class
documentation:
Removes the converter registered for the given classname. Dicts with that classname tag
will be deserialized by the default mechanism again.

methods.name: Pyro5.serializers.SerializerBase.class_to_dict
documentation: Convert a non-serializable object to a dict. Partly borrowed from serpent.

methods.name: Pyro5.serializers.SerializerBase.dict_to_class
documentation:
Recreate an object out of a dict containing the class name and the attributes.
Only a fixed set of classes are recognized.

methods.name: Pyro5.serializers.SerializerBase.__eq__
documentation: this equality method is only to support the unit tests of this class

class_variables.name: Pyro5.serializers.SerializerBase.serializer_id
documentation: define uniquely in subclass
Base class for (de)serializer implementations (which must be thread safe)

classes.name: Pyro5.serializers.SerpentSerializer

class_variables.name: Pyro5.serializers.SerpentSerializer.serializer_id
documentation: never change this
(de)serializer that wraps the serpent serialization protocol.

classes.name: Pyro5.serializers.MarshalSerializer

class_variables.name: Pyro5.serializers.MarshalSerializer.serializer_id
documentation: never change this
(de)serializer that wraps the marshal serialization protocol.

classes.name: Pyro5.serializers.JsonSerializer

class_variables.name: Pyro5.serializers.JsonSerializer.serializer_id
documentation: never change this
(de)serializer that wraps the json serialization protocol.

classes.name: Pyro5.serializers.MsgpackSerializer

class_variables.name: Pyro5.serializers.MsgpackSerializer.serializer_id
documentation: never change this
(de)serializer that wraps the msgpack serialization protocol.

modules.name: Pyro5
documentation:
Pyro package. Some generic init stuff to set up logging etc.

Pyro - Python Remote Objects.  Copyright by Irmen de Jong (irmen@razorvine.net).

functions.name: Pyro5.__configure_logging
documentation:
Do some basic config of the logging module at package import time.
The configuring is done only if the PYRO_LOGLEVEL env var is set.
If you want to use your own logging config, make sure you do
that before any Pyro imports. Then Pyro will skip the autoconfig.
Set the env var PYRO_LOGFILE to change the name of the autoconfigured
log file (default is pyro5.log in the current dir). Use '{stderr}' to
make the log go to the standard error output.

modules.name: Pyro5.client
documentation:
Client related classes (Proxy, mostly)

Pyro - Python Remote Objects.  Copyright by Irmen de Jong (irmen@razorvine.net).

classes.name: Pyro5.client.Proxy

methods.name: Pyro5.client.Proxy.__bool__
documentation: When special methods are invoked via special syntax (e.g. obj[index] calls
obj.__getitem__(index)), the special methods are not looked up via __getattr__
for efficiency reasons; instead, their presence is checked directly.
Thus we need to define them here to force (remote) lookup through __getitem__.

methods.name: Pyro5.client.Proxy._pyroRelease
documentation: release the connection to the pyro daemon

methods.name: Pyro5.client.Proxy._pyroBind
documentation:
Bind this proxy to the exact object from the uri. That means that the proxy's uri
will be updated with a direct PYRO uri, if it isn't one yet.
If the proxy is already bound, it will not bind again.

methods.name: Pyro5.client.Proxy._pyroInvoke
documentation: perform the remote method call communication

methods.name: Pyro5.client.Proxy.__pyroCreateConnection
documentation:
Connects this proxy to the remote Pyro daemon. Does connection handshake.
Returns true if a new connection was made, false if an existing one was already present.

methods.name: Pyro5.client.Proxy._pyroGetMetadata
documentation:
Get metadata from server (methods, attrs, oneway, ...) and remember them in some attributes of the proxy.
Usually this will already be known due to the default behavior of the connect handshake, where the
connect response also includes the metadata.

methods.name: Pyro5.client.Proxy._pyroReconnect
documentation:
(Re)connect the proxy to the daemon containing the pyro object which the proxy is for.
In contrast to the _pyroBind method, this one first releases the connection (if the proxy is still connected)
and retries making a new connection until it succeeds or the given amount of tries ran out.

methods.name: Pyro5.client.Proxy._pyroValidateHandshake
documentation:
Process and validate the initial connection handshake response data received from the daemon.
Simply return without error if everything is ok.
Raise an exception if something is wrong and the connection should not be made.

methods.name: Pyro5.client.Proxy._pyroClaimOwnership
documentation:
The current thread claims the ownership of this proxy from another thread.
Any existing connection will remain active!

methods.name: Pyro5.client.Proxy.__serializeBlobArgs
documentation:
Special handling of a "blob" argument that has to stay serialized until explicitly deserialized in client code.
This makes efficient, transparent gateways or dispatchers and such possible:
they don't have to de/reserialize the message and are independent from the serialized class definitions.
Annotations are passed in because some blob metadata is added. They're not part of the blob itself.

Pyro proxy for a remote object. Intercepts method calls and dispatches them to the remote object.

```
.. automethod:: _pyroBind
```

```
.. automethod:: _pyroRelease
```

```
.. automethod:: _pyroReconnect
```

```
.. automethod:: _pyroValidateHandshake
```

```
.. autoattribute:: _pyroTimeout
```

```
.. attribute:: _pyroMaxRetries

    Number of retries to perform on communication calls by this proxy, allows you to override the default setting.
```

```
.. attribute:: _pyroSerializer

    Name of the serializer to use by this proxy, allows you to override the default setting.
```

```
.. attribute:: _pyroHandshake

    The data object that should be sent in the initial connection handshake message. Can be any serializable object.
```

```
.. attribute:: _pyroLocalSocket

    The socket that is used locally to connect to the remote daemon.
    The format depends on the address family used for the connection, but usually
    for IPV4 connections it is the familiar (hostname, port) tuple.
    Consult the Python documentation on `socket families <https://docs.python.org/3/library/socket.html#socket-families>`_
    for more details
```

classes.name: Pyro5.client._RemoteMethod
documentation: method call abstraction

classes.name: Pyro5.client._StreamResultIterator
documentation:
Pyro returns this as a result of a remote call which returns an iterator or generator.
It is a normal iterable and produces elements on demand from the remote iterator.
You can simply use it in for loops, list comprehensions etc.

classes.name: Pyro5.client._BatchedRemoteMethod
documentation: method call abstraction that is used with batched calls

classes.name: Pyro5.client.BatchProxy
documentation:
Proxy that lets you batch multiple method calls into one.
It is constructed with a reference to the normal proxy that will
carry out the batched calls. Call methods on this object that you want to batch,
and finally call the batch proxy itself. That call will return a generator
for the results of every method call in the batch (in sequence).

classes.name: Pyro5.client.SerializedBlob

methods.name: Pyro5.client.SerializedBlob.deserialized
documentation: Retrieves the client data stored in this blob. Deserializes the data automatically if required.

Used to wrap some data to make Pyro pass this object transparently (it keeps the serialized payload as-is)
Only when you need to access the actual client data you can deserialize on demand.
This makes efficient, transparent gateways or dispatchers and such possible:
they don't have to de/reserialize the message and are independent from the serialized class definitions.
You have to pass this as the only parameter to a remote method call for Pyro to understand it.
Init arguments:
`info` = some (small) descriptive data about the blob. Can be a simple id or name or guid. Must be marshallable.
`data` = the actual client data payload that you want to transfer in the blob. Can be anything that you would
otherwise have used as regular remote call arguments.

modules.name: Pyro5.server
documentation:
Server related classes (Daemon etc)

Pyro - Python Remote Objects.  Copyright by Irmen de Jong (irmen@razorvine.net).

functions.name: Pyro5.server.is_private_attribute
documentation: returns if the attribute name is to be considered private or not.

functions.name: Pyro5.server.callback
documentation:
decorator to mark a method to be a 'callback'. This will make Pyro
raise any errors also on the callback side, and not only on the side
that does the callback call.
decorators

functions.name: Pyro5.server.oneway
documentation: decorator to mark a method to be oneway (client won't wait for a response)

functions.name: Pyro5.server.expose
documentation:
Decorator to mark a method or class to be exposed for remote calls.
You can apply it to a method or a class as a whole.
If you need to change the default instance mode or instance creator, also use a @behavior decorator.

functions.name: Pyro5.server.behavior
documentation: Decorator to specify the server behavior of your Pyro class.

functions.name: Pyro5.server.serve
documentation:
Basic method to fire up a daemon (or supply one yourself).
objects is a dict containing objects to register as keys, and
their names (or None) as values. If ns is true they will be registered
in the naming server as well, otherwise they just stay local.
If you need to publish on a unix domain socket, or require finer control of the daemon's
behavior, you can't use this shortcut method. Create a Daemon yourself and use its
appropriate methods.
See the documentation on 'publishing objects' (in chapter: Servers) for more details.

functions.name: Pyro5.server._default_methodcall_error_handler
documentation: The default routine called to process a exception raised in the user code of a method call

functions.name: Pyro5.server._pyro_obj_to_auto_proxy
documentation: reduce function that automatically replaces Pyro objects by a Proxy

functions.name: Pyro5.server._get_attribute
documentation:
Resolves an attribute name to an object.  Raises
an AttributeError if any attribute in the chain starts with a '`_`'.
Doesn't resolve a dotted name, because that is a security vulnerability.
It treats it as a single attribute name (and the lookup will likely fail).

functions.name: Pyro5.server._reset_exposed_members
documentation: Delete any cached exposed members forcing recalculation on next request

functions.name: Pyro5.server._get_exposed_members
documentation:
Return public and exposed members of the given object's class.
You can also provide a class directly.
Private members are ignored no matter what (names starting with underscore).
If only_exposed is True, only members tagged with the @expose decorator are
returned. If it is False, all public members are returned.
The return value consists of the exposed methods, exposed attributes, and methods
tagged as @oneway.
(All this is used as meta data that Pyro sends to the proxy if it asks for it)

functions.name: Pyro5.server._unpack_weakref
documentation:
Unpack weak reference, or return the object itself, if not a weak reference.
If the weak reference is dead (calling it returns None), raises an
exception. Even though register(...,weak=True) creates finalizer which
will delete the weakref from the mapping, it is possible that the object
is garbage-collected asynchronously between obtaining weakref from the
mapping and reference unpacking, making the weakref invalid; this is handled
by the exception here.

functions.name: Pyro5.server._get_exposed_property_value
documentation:
Return the value of an @exposed @property.
If the requested property is not a @property or not exposed,
an AttributeError is raised instead.

functions.name: Pyro5.server._set_exposed_property_value
documentation:
Sets the value of an @exposed @property.
If the requested property is not a @property or not exposed,
an AttributeError is raised instead.

variables.name: Pyro5.server.__exposed_member_cache
documentation: type: Dict[Tuple[type, bool], Dict[str, Set[str]]]

classes.name: Pyro5.server.DaemonObject

methods.name: Pyro5.server.DaemonObject.registered
documentation: returns a list of all object names registered in this daemon

methods.name: Pyro5.server.DaemonObject.ping
documentation: a simple do-nothing method for testing purposes

methods.name: Pyro5.server.DaemonObject.info
documentation: return some descriptive information about the daemon

methods.name: Pyro5.server.DaemonObject.get_metadata
documentation: Get metadata for the given object (exposed methods, oneways, attributes).
The part of the daemon that is exposed as a Pyro object.

classes.name: Pyro5.server.Daemon

methods.name: Pyro5.server.Daemon.sock
documentation: the server socket used by the daemon

methods.name: Pyro5.server.Daemon.sockets
documentation: list of all sockets used by the daemon (server socket and all active client sockets)

methods.name: Pyro5.server.Daemon.selector
documentation: the multiplexing selector used, if using the multiplex server type

methods.name: Pyro5.server.Daemon.serveSimple
documentation:
Backwards compatibility method to fire up a daemon and start serving requests.
New code should just use the global `serve` function instead.

methods.name: Pyro5.server.Daemon.requestLoop
documentation:
Goes in a loop to service incoming requests, until someone breaks this
or calls shutdown from another thread.

methods.name: Pyro5.server.Daemon.events
documentation: for use in an external event loop: handle any requests that are pending for this daemon

methods.name: Pyro5.server.Daemon.shutdown
documentation: Cleanly terminate a daemon that is running in the requestloop.

methods.name: Pyro5.server.Daemon._handshake
documentation:
Perform connection handshake with new clients.
Client sends a MSG_CONNECT message with a serialized data payload.
If all is well, return with a CONNECT_OK message.
The reason we're not doing this with a MSG_INVOKE method call on the daemon
(like when retrieving the metadata) is because we need to force the clients
to get past an initial connect handshake before letting them invoke any method.
Return True for successful handshake, False if something was wrong.
If a denied_reason is given, the handshake will fail with the given reason.

methods.name: Pyro5.server.Daemon.validateHandshake
documentation:
Override this to create a connection validator for new client connections.
It should return a response data object normally if the connection is okay,
or should raise an exception if the connection should be denied.

methods.name: Pyro5.server.Daemon.clientDisconnect
documentation:
Override this to handle a client disconnect.
Conn is the SocketConnection object that was disconnected.

methods.name: Pyro5.server.Daemon.handleRequest
documentation:
Handle incoming Pyro request. Catches any exception that may occur and
wraps it in a reply to the calling side, as to not make this server side loop
terminate due to exceptions caused by remote invocations.

methods.name: Pyro5.server.Daemon._housekeeping
documentation: Perform periodical housekeeping actions (cleanups etc)

methods.name: Pyro5.server.Daemon.housekeeping
documentation:
Override this to add custom periodic housekeeping (cleanup) logic.
This will be called every few seconds by the running daemon's request loop.

methods.name: Pyro5.server.Daemon._getInstance
documentation: Find or create a new instance of the class

methods.name: Pyro5.server.Daemon._sendExceptionResponse
documentation: send an exception back including the local traceback info

methods.name: Pyro5.server.Daemon.register
documentation:
Register a Pyro object under the given id. Note that this object is now only
known inside this daemon, it is not automatically available in a name server.
This method returns a URI for the registered object.
Pyro checks if an object is already registered, unless you set force=True.
You can register a class or an object (instance) directly.
For a class, Pyro will create instances of it to handle the remote calls according
to the instance_mode (set via @expose on the class). The default there is one object
per session (=proxy connection). If you register an object directly, Pyro will use
that single object for *all* remote calls.
With *weak=True*, only weak reference to the object will be stored, and the object will
get unregistered from the daemon automatically when garbage-collected.

methods.name: Pyro5.server.Daemon.unregister
documentation:
Remove a class or object from the known objects inside this daemon.
You can unregister the class/object directly, or with its id.

methods.name: Pyro5.server.Daemon.uriFor
documentation:
Get a URI for the given object (or object id) from this daemon.
Only a daemon can hand out proper uris because the access location is
contained in them.
Note that unregistered objects cannot be given an uri, but unregistered
object names can (it's just a string we're creating in that case).
If nat is set to False, the configured NAT address (if any) is ignored and it will
return an URI for the internal address.

methods.name: Pyro5.server.Daemon.resetMetadataCache
documentation:
Reset cache of metadata when a Daemon has available methods/attributes
dynamically updated.  Clients will have to get a new proxy to see changes

methods.name: Pyro5.server.Daemon.proxyFor
documentation:
Get a fully initialized Pyro Proxy for the given object (or object id) for this daemon.
If nat is False, the configured NAT address (if any) is ignored.
The object or id must be registered in this daemon, or you'll get an exception.
(you can't get a proxy for an unknown object)

methods.name: Pyro5.server.Daemon.close
documentation: Close down the server and release resources

methods.name: Pyro5.server.Daemon.annotations
documentation: Override to return a dict with custom user annotations to be sent with each response message.

methods.name: Pyro5.server.Daemon.combine
documentation:
Combines the event loop of the other daemon in the current daemon's loop.
You can then simply run the current daemon's requestLoop to serve both daemons.
This works fine on the multiplex server type, but doesn't work with the threaded server type.

Pyro daemon. Contains server side logic and dispatches incoming remote method calls
to the appropriate objects.

modules.name: Pyro5.nsc
documentation:
Name server control tool.

Pyro - Python Remote Objects.  Copyright by Irmen de Jong (irmen@razorvine.net).

modules.name: Pyro5.api
documentation:
Single module that centralizes the main symbols from the Pyro5 API.
It imports most of the other packages that it needs
and provides shortcuts to the most frequently used objects and functions from those packages.
This means you can mostly just `import Pyro5.api` in your code to have access to most of
the Pyro5 objects and functions.

Pyro - Python Remote Objects.  Copyright by Irmen de Jong (<mailto:irmen@razorvine.net>).

modules.name: Pyro5.nameserver
documentation:
Name Server and helper functions.

Pyro - Python Remote Objects.  Copyright by Irmen de Jong (irmen@razorvine.net).

functions.name: Pyro5.nameserver.start_ns_loop
documentation: utility function that starts a new Name server and enters its requestloop.

functions.name: Pyro5.nameserver.start_ns
documentation:
utility fuction to quickly get a Name server daemon to be used in your own event loops.
Returns (nameserverUri, nameserverDaemon, broadcastServer).

functions.name: Pyro5.nameserver.lookup
documentation:
Utility function to call nameserver.lookup,
with the possibility of a retry loop until the asked name becomes available.
You have to set the delay_time (or the corresponding config item)
to the maximum number of seconds you are willing to wait.

functions.name: Pyro5.nameserver.yplookup
documentation:
Utility function to call nameserver.yplookup,
with the possibility of a retry loop until the asked name becomes available.
You have to set the delay_time (or the corresponding config item)
to the maximum number of seconds you are willing to wait.

classes.name: Pyro5.nameserver.MemoryStorage
documentation:
Storage implementation that is just an in-memory dict.
(because it inherits from dict it is automatically a collections.MutableMapping)
Stopping the nameserver will make the server instantly forget about everything.

classes.name: Pyro5.nameserver.SqlStorage
documentation:
Sqlite-based storage.
It is just a single (name,uri) table for the names and another table for the metadata.
Sqlite db connection objects aren't thread-safe, so a new connection is created in every method.

classes.name: Pyro5.nameserver.NameServer

methods.name: Pyro5.nameserver.NameServer.count
documentation: Returns the number of name registrations.

methods.name: Pyro5.nameserver.NameServer.lookup
documentation:
Lookup the given name, returns an URI if found.
Returns tuple (uri, metadata) if return_metadata is True.

methods.name: Pyro5.nameserver.NameServer.register
documentation:
Register a name with an URI. If safe is true, name cannot be registered twice.
The uri can be a string or an URI object. Metadata must be None, or a collection of strings.

methods.name: Pyro5.nameserver.NameServer.set_metadata

Pyro name server. Provides a simple flat name space to map logical object names to Pyro URIs.
Default storage is done in an in-memory dictionary. You can provide custom storage types.

modules.name: Pyro5.core
documentation:
Multi purpose stuff used by both clients and servers (URI etc)

Pyro - Python Remote Objects.  Copyright by Irmen de Jong (irmen@razorvine.net).

functions.name: Pyro5.core.resolve
documentation:
Resolve a 'magic' uri (PYRONAME, PYROMETA) into the direct PYRO uri.
It finds a name server, and use that to resolve a PYRONAME uri into the direct PYRO uri pointing to the named object.
If uri is already a PYRO uri, it is returned unmodified.
You can consider this a shortcut function so that you don't have to locate and use a name server proxy yourself.
Note: if you need to resolve more than a few names, consider using the name server directly instead of repeatedly
calling this function, to avoid the name server lookup overhead from each call.
You can set delay_time to the maximum number of seconds you are prepared to wait until a name registration
becomes available in the nameserver.

functions.name: Pyro5.core.locate_ns
documentation: Get a proxy for a name server somewhere in the network.

functions.name: Pyro5.core.type_meta
documentation: extracts type metadata from the given class or object, can be used as Name server metadata.

classes.name: Pyro5.core.URI

methods.name: Pyro5.core.URI.isUnixsockLocation
documentation: determine if a location string is for a Unix domain socket

methods.name: Pyro5.core.URI.location
documentation: property containing the location string, for instance ``"servername.you.com:5555"``

Pyro object URI (universal resource identifier).
The uri format is like this: `PYRO:objectid@location` where location is one of:

- `hostname:port` (tcp/ip socket on given port)
- `./u:sockname` (Unix domain socket on localhost)

There is also a 'Magic format' for simple name resolution using Name server:

: `PYRONAME:objectname[@location]`  (optional name server location, can also omit location port)

And one that looks up things in the name server by metadata:

: `PYROMETA:meta1,meta2,...[@location]`  (optional name server location, can also omit location port)

You can write the protocol in lowercase if you like (`pyro:...`) but it will
automatically be converted to uppercase internally.

classes.name: Pyro5.core._ExceptionWrapper

methods.name: Pyro5.core._ExceptionWrapper.__serialized_dict__
documentation: serialized form as a dictionary

Class that wraps a remote exception. If this is returned, Pyro will
re-throw the exception on the receiving side. Usually this is taken care of
by a special response message flag, but in the case of batched calls this
flag is useless and another mechanism was needed.

modules.name: Pyro5.protocol
documentation:
The pyro wire protocol structures.

Pyro - Python Remote Objects.  Copyright by Irmen de Jong (<mailto:irmen@razorvine.net>).

Wire messages contains of a fixed size header, an optional set of annotation chunks,
and then the payload data. This class doesn't deal with the payload data:
(de)serialization and handling of that data is done elsewhere.
Annotation chunks are only parsed.

The header format is:

```
0x00   4s  4   'PYRO' (message identifier)
0x04   H   2   protocol version
0x06   B   1   message type
0x07   B   1   serializer id
0x08   H   2   message flags
0x0a   H   2   sequence number   (to identify proper request-reply sequencing)
0x0c   I   4   data length   (max 4 Gb)
0x10   I   4   annotations length (max 4 Gb, total of all chunks, 0 if no annotation chunks present)
0x14   16s 16  correlation uuid
0x24   H   2   (reserved)
0x26   H   2   magic number 0x4dc5
total size: 0x28 (40 bytes)
```

After the header, zero or more annotation chunks may follow, of the format:

```
4s  4   annotation id (4 ASCII letters)
I   4   chunk length  (max 4 Gb)
B   x   annotation chunk databytes
```

After that, the actual payload data bytes follow.

modules.name: Pyro5.compatibility.Pyro4
documentation:
An effort to provide a backward-compatible Pyro4 API layer,
to make porting existing code from Pyro4 to Pyro5 easier.

This only works for code that imported Pyro4 symbols from the Pyro4 module
directly, instead of from one of Pyro4's sub modules. So, for instance:
from Pyro4 import Proxy  instead of:  from Pyro4.core import Proxy

*some* submodules are more or less emulated such as Pyro4.errors, Pyro4.socketutil.

So, you may first have to convert your old code to use the importing scheme to
only import the Pyro4 module and not from its submodules, and then you should
insert this at the top to enable the compatibility layer:
from Pyro5.compatibility import Pyro4


Pyro - Python Remote Objects.  Copyright by Irmen de Jong (irmen@razorvine.net).

functions.name: Pyro5.compatibility.Pyro4.asyncproxy
documentation: symbols that are no longer available in Pyro5 and that we don't emulate:

modules.name: Pyro5.socketutil
documentation:
Low level socket utilities.

Pyro - Python Remote Objects.  Copyright by Irmen de Jong (irmen@razorvine.net).

functions.name: Pyro5.socketutil.get_ip_address
documentation:
Returns the IP address for the given host. If you enable the workaround,
it will use a little hack if the ip address is found to be the loopback address.
The hack tries to discover an externally visible ip address instead (this only works for ipv4 addresses).
Set ipVersion=6 to return ipv6 addresses, 4 to return ipv4, 0 to let OS choose the best one or None to use config.PREFER_IP_VERSION.

functions.name: Pyro5.socketutil.get_interface
documentation: tries to find the network interface that connects to the given host's address

functions.name: Pyro5.socketutil.receive_data
documentation:
Retrieve a given number of bytes from a socket.
It is expected the socket is able to supply that number of bytes.
If it isn't, an exception is raised (you will not get a zero length result
or a result that is smaller than what you asked for). The partial data that
has been received however is stored in the 'partialData' attribute of
the exception object.

functions.name: Pyro5.socketutil.send_data
documentation:
Send some data over a socket.
Some systems have problems with `sendall()` when the socket is in non-blocking mode.
For instance, Mac OS X seems to be happy to throw EAGAIN errors too often.
This function falls back to using a regular send loop if needed.

functions.name: Pyro5.socketutil.create_socket
documentation:
Create a socket. Default socket options are keepalive and IPv4 family, and nodelay (nagle disabled).
If 'bind' or 'connect' is a string, it is assumed a Unix domain socket is requested.
Otherwise, a normal tcp/ip socket tuple (addr, port, ...) is used.
Set ipv6=True to create an IPv6 socket rather than IPv4.
Set ipv6=None to use the PREFER_IP_VERSION config setting.

functions.name: Pyro5.socketutil.create_bc_socket
documentation:
Create a udp broadcast socket.
Set ipv6=True to create an IPv6 socket rather than IPv4.
Set ipv6=None to use the PREFER_IP_VERSION config setting.

functions.name: Pyro5.socketutil.set_reuseaddr
documentation: sets the SO_REUSEADDR option on the socket, if possible.

functions.name: Pyro5.socketutil.set_nodelay
documentation: sets the TCP_NODELAY option on the socket (to disable Nagle's algorithm), if possible.

functions.name: Pyro5.socketutil.set_keepalive
documentation: sets the SO_KEEPALIVE option on the socket, if possible.

functions.name: Pyro5.socketutil.set_noinherit
documentation: Mark the given socket fd as non-inheritable to child processes

functions.name: Pyro5.socketutil.set_noinherit
documentation: Mark the given socket fd as non-inheritable to child processes

functions.name: Pyro5.socketutil.set_noinherit

modules.name: Pyro5.utils.httpgateway
documentation:
HTTP gateway: connects the web browser's world of javascript+http and Pyro.
Creates a stateless HTTP server that essentially is a proxy for the Pyro objects behind it.
It exposes the Pyro objects through a HTTP interface and uses the JSON serializer,
so that you can immediately process the response data in the browser.

You can start this module as a script from the command line, to easily get a
http gateway server running:

> {command}`python -m Pyro5.utils.httpgateway`
> or simply: {command}`pyro5-httpgateway`

It is also possible to import the 'pyro_app' function and stick that into a WSGI
server of your choice, to have more control.

The javascript code in the web page of the gateway server works with the same-origin
browser policy because it is served by the gateway itself. If you want to access it
from scripts in different sites, you have to work around this or embed the gateway app
in your site. Non-browser clients that access the http api have no problems.
See the `http` example for two of such clients (node.js and python).

Pyro - Python Remote Objects.  Copyright by Irmen de Jong (<mailto:irmen@razorvine.net>).

documentation: Called if invalid http method.

documentation: OPTION Call with CORS

documentation: Called if Url not found.

functions.name: Pyro5.utils.httpgateway.pyro_app
documentation:
The WSGI app function that is used to process the requests.
You can stick this into a wsgi server of your choice, or use the main() method
to use the default wsgiref server.

functions.name: Pyro5.utils.httpgateway.singlyfy_parameters

Makes a parsed querystring parameter dictionary into a dict where the values that
